stem: The integral test compares the p-series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{[[C]]}{n^{[[p]]}} \) to the improper integral \( \displaystyle\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{[[C]]}{x^{[[p]]}}\,dx \). Since \( p=[[p]]>1 \), this companion integral converges (confirming the series converges). Compute the value of the integral.
solution: By the power rule, \( \int_{1}^{\infty} [[C]]x^{-[[p]]}\,dx = \left[\dfrac{[[C]]x^{1-[[p]]}}{1-[[p]]}\right]_{1}^{\infty} = \dfrac{[[C]]}{[[p]]-1} = [[answer]] \), since \( x^{1-[[p]]}\to 0 \) as \( x\to\infty \) (because \( p>1 \)).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( p \) instead of \( p-1 \), forgetting the antiderivative shifts the exponent by one.
This multiplies by \( p-1 \) instead of dividing, inverting the correct relationship.
This divides by \( p+1 \), using the wrong sign on the exponent shift.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The convergent p-series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{[[p]]}} \) has tail \( R_{[[N]]} = \displaystyle\sum_{n=[[N]]+1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{[[p]]}} \). The integral test gives the bound \( R_{[[N]]} \le \displaystyle\int_{[[N]]}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{x^{[[p]]}} \). Compute this upper bound for \( N=[[N]] \).
solution: By the power rule, \( \displaystyle\int_{[[N]]}^{\infty} x^{-[[p]]}\,dx = \left[\dfrac{x^{1-[[p]]}}{1-[[p]]}\right]_{[[N]]}^{\infty} = \dfrac{[[N]]^{1-[[p]]}}{[[p]]-1} = [[answer]] \), since \( x^{1-[[p]]}\to 0 \) as \( x\to\infty \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the \( \frac{1}{p-1} \) prefactor that comes from integrating the power function.
This raises \( N \) to the \( p \) power instead of \( p-1 \), an off-by-one error in the exponent.
This uses \( N^{-p} \), the value of the series' \( N \)th term itself, not the integral bound.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This inverts the role of \( p-1 \), multiplying instead of dividing.
